memory.summary: Teammate A is asking me to take over their part because they have an emergency, which feels like a test of our trust. I'm tempted to just do it since we're close friends and I value the relationship more than sticking rigidly to my own plan.
They're asking nicely, but dumping their work on me feels like a breach of trust even if I value our bond. I need to set a boundary now before my stress spikes.
I swallowed my pride and took their work to save our friendship, but now I'm drowning. My stress is spiking because I just violated my own boundaries again.
I swallowed my pride to save our friendship, but now I'm drowning under their unfinished work. My stress is spiking because I violated my own boundaries again.
I just missed the deadline for both my work and theirs because I swallowed my pride again. My stress is spiking, but I'm terrified that pushing back will finally break our friendship.
I swallowed my pride again to save our friendship, but now I'm drowning under their unfinished work while my stress spikes. Violating my boundaries feels safer than risking the relationship.
I'm drowning again because I swallowed my pride to save the friendship, proving that avoiding conflict just dumps more work on me while my stress spikes.
Swallowing my pride just dumped their unfinished work on me, making my load heavier and proving that avoiding conflict only hurts me more.
I swallowed my pride again to save the friendship, but now I'm drowning under their unfinished work while my stress spikes. Violating my boundaries feels safer than risking the relationship.
Pushing back didn't hurt our trust; teammate_A actually asked how to split the load, proving that protecting my boundaries is safer than swallowing my pride.

Directly confronting teammate_A worked perfectly; they accepted the boundary and apologized without any damage to our trust. Swallowing my pride was unnecessary pain when assertiveness actually strengthens our partnership.
I spent 1.5 hours fixing their mess again, and my stress just spiked higher. Swallowing my pride to save face is costing me more energy than I have left.

desires: Stop feeling like a doormat; I need to prove that saying 'no' or setting a hard boundary actually protects my friendship instead of destroying it.
Reclaim my own time and mental space immediately so I don't burn out before the deadline hits.
Make teammate_A feel the full weight of their own unfinished work without me stepping in to bail them out again.

goals.description: Stop overthinking the text. I sent it, they said 'I'll figure it out,' and that's enough proof my boundary worked. No more drafting replies or analyzing their tone.
goals.rationale: My impulsive nature wants to keep fixing things immediately, but my affiliation value knows that obsessing now only hurts our closeness. They accepted the new arrangement; pushing harder just creates unnecessary friction. I need to trust the outcome so I can actually rest.

goals.description: Get into bed and shut off the laptop. The work is paused until Monday at 19:00; doing anything else now is just feeding my procrastination bias while burning out.
goals.rationale: With sleep discipline already low and energy drained, continuing to stare at screens contradicts my health values. My short planning horizon means I can't afford a crash tomorrow. I'm deferring this because I've been too busy fighting internal conflict to prioritize rest.

agent.last_reasoning: I just worked until 4:30 AM. My energy is zero and sleep debt is critical. Pushing harder now feeds my procrastination bias and risks a crash tomorrow. I need to shut down, get rest, and tackle the remaining work Monday at 19:00 as planned.

goals.rationale: My affiliation value (0.5) outweighs my health or growth needs right now. I can't let them feel like a burden; absorbing this shock is how I maintain trust in our core relationship.
